intake_questions.text: List all business units that collect or process personal data of Indian residents.
Which categories of personal data are processed? (name, contact, financial, health, biometric, children's data, location, employment)
Do you transfer personal data outside India? If yes, to which countries and to which processors?
Are you a Significant Data Fiduciary? (>1 crore data principals, or processing sensitive data at scale, or designated by Central Government)
What is the primary lawful basis for processing: consent or legitimate use? Specify per processing activity.
Which sector best describes your organisation? (Healthcare, Manufacturing, Automation/Technology, Financial Services, Education/EdTech, E-Commerce/Retail, Telecom, Government/Public Sector, Other)
Do you process personal data of children (under 18)? If yes, describe the age verification and parental consent mechanism.
How many data principals does your organisation process personal data for (approximate)? This determines SDF threshold assessment.

scope_template.prompt: Identify each business unit processing personal data of Indian residents
List all systems, applications, and databases storing or transmitting personal data
Identify all data processors (third-party vendors handling personal data on your behalf)
Identify all cross-border personal data transfers including cloud providers
Confirm any systems explicitly excluded from DPDP scope with justification

requirements.text: A notice must be provided to data principals at or before collection stating: personal data to be collected, purpose, how to exercise rights, and how to complain to DPBI.
requirements.evidence_expectation: Current privacy notice document; screenshot of notice at all collection points (web, app, paper forms, APIs)

requirements.text: The notice must be available in English and at least one Eighth Schedule language upon request.
requirements.evidence_expectation: Language versions of the privacy notice; process documentation for responding to translated notice requests

requirements.text: For personal data collected prior to the Act, a notice must have been provided at the time of collection or re-issued within the prescribed transition period.
requirements.evidence_expectation: Evidence of legacy notice re-issuance or confirmation of pre-Act collection notices; transition plan documentation

requirements.text: Consent must be free, specific, informed, unconditional, and unambiguous via a clear affirmative action. No pre-ticked boxes, bundled consents, or deceptive patterns.
requirements.evidence_expectation: Consent UI screenshots; consent log samples; dark pattern assessment; affirmative action mechanism description

requirements.text: Data principals must be able to withdraw consent as easily as it was given. Withdrawal must trigger cessation of processing not covered by legitimate use.
requirements.evidence_expectation: Consent withdrawal UI or API; withdrawal audit log; evidence processing stops after withdrawal; no dark patterns blocking withdrawal

requirements.text: Consent records must be maintained demonstrating who consented, when, for which specific purpose, and the mechanism used.
requirements.evidence_expectation: Consent database schema or log extract with timestamp, purpose, and data principal identity; consent record retention policy

requirements.text: Personal data must only be processed for the specific purpose for which consent was given or legitimate use applies. Secondary use requires fresh consent.
requirements.evidence_expectation: Records of Processing Activities (RoPA) linking each data element to a lawful basis; evidence no secondary use without consent

requirements.text: Personal data must not be retained beyond the period necessary for the stated purpose or as mandated by law. Deletion or anonymisation must be triggered at end of retention.
requirements.evidence_expectation: Data retention schedule per category and sector law; automated deletion or archival policy; deletion audit logs

requirements.text: Data principals have the right to access a summary of personal data being processed, identities of processors, and data shared with other fiduciaries.
requirements.evidence_expectation: Access request SOP; technical mechanism for providing data summary; SLA; sample fulfilled request (anonymised)

requirements.text: Data principals have the right to correction, completion, updating, and erasure of personal data. Requests must be fulfilled within prescribed SLA.
requirements.evidence_expectation: Correction/erasure workflow documentation; technical deletion mechanism with cascading to processors; SLA tracking records

requirements.text: A grievance redressal mechanism must be established with a designated officer whose contact details are publicly accessible. Grievances must be addressed within prescribed period.
requirements.evidence_expectation: Grievance officer designation; public-facing contact; portal or email; SLA; sample resolved grievances with response times

requirements.text: Reasonable security safeguards must be implemented to prevent personal data breach, including encryption, access controls, monitoring, and regular security testing.
requirements.evidence_expectation: Information security policy; encryption evidence for PII at rest and in transit; access control records; pentest report (last 12 months)

requirements.text: All data processors must be bound by written Data Processing Agreements including security, purpose limitation, and breach notification obligations.
requirements.evidence_expectation: Signed DPAs with all data processors; processor security assessment records; DPA breach notification clause

requirements.text: In case of a personal data breach, the DPBI and affected data principals must be notified without delay in the prescribed manner.
requirements.evidence_expectation: Incident response plan with breach notification SOP; DPBI notification templates; data principal notification capability; tabletop exercise record

requirements.text: Personal data must only be transferred outside India to countries on the Central Government approved list, with appropriate contractual safeguards for foreign processors.
requirements.evidence_expectation: Cross-border transfer mapping; receiving country compliance assessment; DPAs with foreign processors including DPDP protections

requirements.text: A Data Protection Officer or point of contact must be designated. For Significant Data Fiduciaries, the DPO must report to the board and be based in India.
requirements.evidence_expectation: DPO appointment letter; public-facing DPO contact; board reporting evidence (for SDF); India residency evidence (for SDF)

requirements.text: Significant Data Fiduciaries must conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIA) for high-risk processing activities including AI/ML, large-scale biometrics, and health data.
requirements.evidence_expectation: SDF threshold assessment; DPIA methodology; completed DPIAs for qualifying activities; DPBI reporting records

requirements.text: Before processing personal data of a child (under 18), verifiable parental or guardian consent must be obtained. Children's data must not be used for tracking, profiling, or behavioural advertising.
requirements.evidence_expectation: Age verification mechanism; parental consent flow; technical age gate; evidence children's data excluded from profiling and advertising

evidence_requests.description: Current privacy notice shown at all collection points including web, mobile app, and API responses

evidence_requests.title: Multilingual notice evidence

evidence_requests.description: Evidence of pre-Act data notice re-issuance or transition plan

evidence_requests.title: Consent mechanism screenshots and dark pattern assessment
evidence_requests.description: Screenshots of consent UI, dark pattern review evidence, and affirmative action confirmation

evidence_requests.title: Consent withdrawal mechanism demo
evidence_requests.description: Demo or docs of withdrawal process, audit log showing withdrawal processed, downstream cessation evidence

evidence_requests.description: Sample consent log showing who, when, purpose, mechanism; retention policy for consent records

evidence_requests.title: Records of Processing Activities (RoPA)
evidence_requests.description: Full RoPA linking each data element, purpose, lawful basis, retention period, and processors

evidence_requests.title: Data retention schedule and deletion evidence
evidence_requests.description: Retention schedule per data category; automated deletion configuration; sample deletion audit logs

evidence_requests.title: Data access request SOP and sample
evidence_requests.description: SOP for access requests, technical mechanism, SLA, and an anonymised sample fulfilled request

evidence_requests.title: Erasure and correction workflow
evidence_requests.description: Technical and procedural documentation for erasure and correction, cascading deletion to processors

evidence_requests.title: Grievance portal, SLA, and sample resolutions
evidence_requests.description: Grievance officer details, portal/email, SLA documentation, and sample resolved grievances

evidence_requests.title: Security safeguards evidence pack
evidence_requests.description: IS policy, encryption configuration, access control records, MFA evidence, latest pentest report

evidence_requests.title: Data Processing Agreements with all processors
evidence_requests.description: Signed DPAs with all vendors handling personal data; processor security questionnaire results

evidence_requests.title: Incident response and breach notification plan
evidence_requests.description: IRP with DPBI notification SOP, notification templates, tabletop exercise record

evidence_requests.title: Cross-border transfer mapping and safeguards
evidence_requests.description: Data flow diagram showing international transfers; receiving country list; foreign processor DPAs

evidence_requests.title: DPO appointment and public contact evidence
evidence_requests.description: DPO designation record, public-facing contact page, board reporting evidence

evidence_requests.title: DPIA records and SDF threshold assessment
evidence_requests.description: SDF determination document; DPIA methodology; completed DPIAs for qualifying processing activities

evidence_requests.title: Children's data controls and parental consent mechanism
evidence_requests.description: Age verification system docs; parental consent flow; evidence children excluded from profiling/advertising

task_templates.title: DPO/CISO interview — data processing overview, lawful basis register, and governance structure
Legal/compliance interview — consent architecture, rights fulfilment SLAs, and grievance handling
IT/engineering interview — data systems inventory, encryption, access controls, and deletion automation
Data mapping workshop — personal data flows, RoPA construction, and cross-border transfer identification
Sector-specific risk workshop — industry data types, sector law overlaps, and residual risk assessment
Privacy notice and consent mechanism review — dark pattern check and language accessibility test
Security safeguards and DPA review — processor inventory, DPA completeness, and security controls
Breach response and grievance process review — IRP DPDP alignment and SLA assessment
Children's data controls review — age gate testing and parental consent verification
Cross-border transfer review — transfer mapping, approved country list compliance, and foreign DPAs
Consent withdrawal and access request functional test — end-to-end rights exercise
Erasure cascade test — verify deletion propagates to all downstream processors
DPIA facilitation — high-risk processing activities identification and impact assessment

severity_model.critical: Fundamental legal violation: processing without any lawful basis, complete absence of consent mechanism, total failure to respond to data principal rights, or no DPBI breach notification capability — creates immediate enforcement risk and potential DPBI penalty.
severity_model.high: Significant DPDP non-compliance: materially deficient privacy notice, inadequate consent records, no DPAs with processors, missing breach notification SOP, or SDF obligations (DPIA/DPO) not met.
severity_model.medium: Partial compliance gap: notice missing required elements, consent withdrawal difficult, rights fulfilment process has SLA gaps, security safeguards partially implemented, or cross-border transfers not fully mapped.
severity_model.low: Minor procedural gap: notice language improvements needed, consent records incomplete for historical data, retention schedules not fully documented, or governance documentation requires updating.
severity_model.informational: Best-practice enhancement with no immediate legal risk: improved consent UX, enhanced grievance portal, stronger encryption, or proactive DPIA for lower-risk processing.

review_gates.label: Scope, data inventory, and sector classification approved
All evidence requests issued and processor DPA inventory complete
Evidence collection complete or exceptions approved
Findings reviewed, sector overlays assessed, gap severity confirmed
Final readiness report approved for delivery
Remediation roadmap accepted and governance plan agreed

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Consent Architecture Design Workshop
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Review current consent collection, storage, and withdrawal mechanisms. Map against DPDP Act Section 6 requirements. Identify dark patterns, bundled consents, and forced consent scenarios. Design a compliant consent management flow with full audit trail. Output: consent implementation specification and technical requirements.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Data Principal Rights Fulfilment Process Design
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Walk through access, correction, and erasure request handling. Define automated vs manual steps, SLA tracking, and escalation paths. Map cascading deletion to all processors. Produce rights fulfilment workflow with RACI chart and system integration requirements.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: DPO Appointment and Governance Framework Advisory
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Review DPO role requirements for Significant Data Fiduciaries. Advise on appointment, independence, board reporting structure, and India residency. Design ongoing DPDP compliance monitoring programme including periodic DPIA review cycles.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Personal Data Security Architecture Review
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Review encryption at rest and in transit for personal data systems. Assess access control, tokenisation, pseudonymisation, and data masking. Map security controls against DPDP security safeguards obligation. Produce security gap remediation roadmap prioritised by personal data criticality.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Breach Detection and DPBI Notification Readiness
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Review breach detection capabilities (SIEM, DLP, anomaly detection). Walk through DPBI notification timeline and content requirements. Design data principal notification playbook at scale. Conduct tabletop exercise simulating a breach scenario with DPDP notification obligations.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Cross-Border Transfer Compliance Programme
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Map all international personal data flows including cloud providers, SaaS tools, and offshore processors. Assess receiving country approval status. Design DPA templates for foreign processors. Establish transfer impact assessment process for high-risk destinations.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: RoPA and Data Minimisation Workshop
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Facilitate construction of the Records of Processing Activities. Identify data minimisation opportunities per system. Review retention schedules against legal requirements for each sector. Design automated deletion pipeline. Output: compliant RoPA template and retention automation specification.

healthcare.label: Healthcare
healthcare.description: Hospitals, clinics, telemedicine platforms, diagnostic labs, health insurance companies, and medical device manufacturers processing patient health data under the DPDP Act. Health data is sensitive personal data under Indian law and attracts heightened obligations.
sensitive_data_types: Patient health records (PHR)
Electronic Medical Records (EMR)
Diagnostic reports and imaging (PACS/DICOM)
Prescription history
Insurance claims and underwriting data
Biometric data (fingerprint for patient identification)
Genetic data
Mental health records
Telemedicine session recordings
healthcare.risk_notes: Health data is among the most sensitive categories under DPDP. Patients have limited bargaining power when consenting to data processing as a condition of treatment, raising concerns about truly free consent. Sector regulators (IRDAI for insurers) may impose additional obligations beyond DPDP.

additional_requirements.text: Patient consent for health data processing must be granular — separate consent for treatment, insurance claims, research use, and sharing with other providers. Generic 'I agree to terms' consent is insufficient for health data.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Granular consent forms or digital consent screens; purpose-specific consent records per patient; consent for research use maintained separately from treatment consent
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.6 + MoHFW Health Data Management Policy

additional_requirements.text: Electronic Medical Records (EMR) and diagnostic imaging systems must implement role-based access control ensuring only treating clinicians can access patient records. EMR access logs must be maintained and reviewed.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: EMR system RBAC configuration; access log samples; privileged access review records; evidence clinician access limited to their patients

additional_requirements.text: Telemedicine consultations must include explicit consent for session recording, data sharing with pharmacy partners, and insurance processing before the consultation begins.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Telemedicine pre-consultation consent screen; recording disclosure; separate consent for third-party sharing; ABDM health ID linkage consent
additional_requirements.sector_law: Telemedicine Practice Guidelines 2020 + DPDP Act S.6

additional_requirements.text: Health data sharing with insurance companies, diagnostic labs, and third-party platforms must be governed by written DPAs. Insurers must not use health data for purposes beyond the agreed claim processing.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: DPAs with insurers, labs, and third-party health apps; purpose limitation clauses in all health data sharing agreements; audit of data use by processors

additional_requirements.text: Patient health data must not be used for secondary purposes such as pharma marketing, research, or AI model training without explicit separate consent. Anonymised or de-identified data used for research must meet DPDP de-identification standards.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Research data use consent records; de-identification methodology documentation; evidence anonymised datasets cannot be re-identified; pharma marketing data separation
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.8(1) + ICMR research ethics guidelines

additional_requirements.text: Patients must be able to access, correct, and transfer their health records in machine-readable format. ABDM Health ID linked records must support data portability to other providers.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Patient data access portal; FHIR/HL7 export capability; ABDM Health ID integration for portability; SLA for health record access requests
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.11-12 + ABDM FHIR guidelines

additional_requirements.text: For paediatric patients, all health data processing must be authorised by a parent or legal guardian. Minor patient records must be segregated and have additional access restrictions.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Paediatric patient consent forms signed by guardian; age flag in patient management system; restricted access to minor records; guardian notification for data requests
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.9 + Clinical Establishments Act

additional_requirements.text: A health data breach (e.g. patient records exposed) must be treated as a critical incident with DPBI notification and immediate patient notification given the sensitivity of health information.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Health data breach classification in IRP; immediate DPBI notification playbook; patient notification template for health breaches; escalation to medical director

intake_questions: What EMR/HIS (Hospital Information System) platforms are in use and do they support ABDM integration?
Do you share patient data with insurance companies, diagnostic labs, or pharmacy networks? If yes, are DPAs in place?
Do you use patient data for AI/ML model training, research, or pharma analytics? If yes, is separate consent obtained?
Do you operate a telemedicine platform? If yes, how is pre-consultation consent captured?
Do you process data of paediatric patients? If yes, describe the guardian consent mechanism.
Are you NABH accredited? If yes, what privacy-related requirements apply under NABH standards?

evidence_requests.title: Granular patient consent forms (treatment / insurance / research)
evidence_requests.description: All patient consent forms showing separate consent per purpose category

evidence_requests.title: EMR RBAC configuration and access logs
evidence_requests.description: EMR system role matrix, access logs, and last access review records

evidence_requests.title: Telemedicine pre-consultation consent screenshot
evidence_requests.description: Screenshot of consent screen shown before telemedicine session with recording and sharing disclosures

evidence_requests.title: Health data processor DPAs (insurers, labs, apps)
evidence_requests.description: DPAs with all health data processors including insurance companies, diagnostic labs, and third-party health platforms

evidence_requests.title: Research data use consent and de-identification methodology
evidence_requests.description: Separate research consent records and de-identification methodology documentation

evidence_requests.title: Paediatric patient guardian consent records
evidence_requests.description: Sample guardian consent forms and evidence of paediatric record access restrictions

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Patient Consent Architecture for EMR and Telemedicine
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Design granular consent flows for treatment, insurance, research, and ABDM linkage. Map consent to DPDP Act Section 6 and MoHFW guidelines. Review existing consent forms for completeness and dark patterns. Produce consent management specification for EMR integration.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Medical Data De-identification for Research and Analytics
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Review de-identification methodology against ICMR and DPDP standards. Assess re-identification risk for shared datasets. Design safe harbour criteria for health data research use. Produce de-identification policy and technical implementation guide.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Healthcare Data Breach Response and DPBI Notification
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Conduct tabletop exercise simulating patient data breach scenario. Review DPBI notification requirements and patient communication at scale. Design breach response playbook specific to health data sensitivity. Include medical director escalation and regulator liaison protocols.

manufacturing.label: Manufacturing
manufacturing.description: Factories, production plants, supply chain operations, and industrial companies processing employee data, contractor data, and visitor records. Key concerns include biometric attendance systems, CCTV surveillance, and OT/IoT device data.
sensitive_data_types: Employee biometric data (fingerprint, face for attendance)
Worker productivity and performance monitoring data
CCTV footage from production floors, entry points
Safety incident and near-miss records
Medical fitness records (pre-employment and periodic)
Contractor and visitor personal data
Union membership data
Supply chain partner employee data
manufacturing.risk_notes: Employees have limited ability to refuse consent where processing is tied to employment terms. Manufacturing environments often collect biometric data at scale for attendance without adequate notice. OT/ICS systems may incidentally collect personal data through worker activity monitoring. Labour law obligations around employment data may extend retention requirements beyond what DPDP permits.

additional_requirements.text: Employee biometric data (fingerprint, face recognition) collected for attendance must be disclosed in the employment contract or a separate biometric data notice. Employees must be informed of storage location, access controls, and retention period.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Employment contract biometric clause or standalone biometric consent form; biometric system vendor DPA; biometric data retention schedule; deletion mechanism at employment end

additional_requirements.text: CCTV surveillance notices must be prominently displayed at all camera locations across the facility. The notice must state the purpose (safety/security), retention period, and who has access to footage.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: CCTV notice boards at all camera locations; CCTV policy with purpose, retention, and access definition; evidence cameras not installed in prohibited areas (restrooms, changing areas)
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.5 + Factories Act S.11A

additional_requirements.text: Contract labour processed through third-party contractors must be covered by data processing agreements. The principal employer remains accountable for personal data processed by contract labour agencies on its behalf.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: DPAs with all contract labour agencies; evidence agencies comply with DPDP obligations; contractor data handling induction records
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.8(8) + Contract Labour Act

additional_requirements.text: Worker productivity monitoring systems (keyloggers, machine output tracking, real-time location within facility) must be disclosed in employment terms. Data must be used only for stated safety or quality purposes, not for individual performance profiling without separate notice.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Employment terms disclosing monitoring systems; productivity monitoring policy; evidence monitoring data not used for individual profiling without separate disclosure

additional_requirements.text: OT/ICS systems that interface with employee access control, safety systems, or HR integrations must be assessed for personal data flows. Personal data must not traverse unsecured OT networks.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: OT/IT network segmentation evidence; personal data flow assessment for OT-connected systems; access control for OT systems handling employee data

additional_requirements.text: Workers must be able to access, correct, and request erasure of their personal data upon employment termination. Biometric data must be deleted within prescribed period after offboarding.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Offboarding data deletion checklist including biometric systems; sample fulfilled employee data access request; erasure SLA for ex-employees
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.12 + Industrial Disputes Act

intake_questions: What biometric systems are deployed (fingerprint attendance, face recognition, access control)?
How many CCTV cameras are installed, and are notices displayed at all locations?
Do you use contract labour agencies? Are DPAs in place with them?
What productivity monitoring or real-time worker tracking systems are deployed on the shop floor?
Do OT/ICS systems (SCADA, PLC) interface with HR, access control, or payroll systems?
What is the employee biometric data deletion procedure at end of employment?

evidence_requests.title: Biometric data consent clause and DPA with biometric vendor
evidence_requests.description: Employment contract biometric clause or separate consent; biometric system vendor DPA and retention schedule

evidence_requests.title: CCTV notice evidence and surveillance policy
evidence_requests.description: Photos of CCTV notices at camera locations; CCTV policy with purpose, retention, and access controls

evidence_requests.title: Contract labour agency DPAs
evidence_requests.description: DPAs with all contract labour agencies covering DPDP obligations

evidence_requests.title: Offboarding data deletion checklist and biometric erasure evidence
evidence_requests.description: Offboarding procedure showing biometric deletion step and sample deletion records

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Biometric Data Governance for Factory Attendance Systems
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Review biometric data collection consent across attendance and access control systems. Map to DPDP Act Section 6 requirements. Design biometric data governance policy covering enrolment, storage, access, and deletion. Produce DPDP-compliant biometric notice templates for employment contracts.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Worker Surveillance and Monitoring Policy Design
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Audit all monitoring systems (CCTV, productivity tracking, machine output, location within facility). Design compliant surveillance policy with purpose limitation and proportionality assessment. Create CCTV notice templates. Establish monitoring data access controls and retention schedules aligned to labour law.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Supply Chain and Contract Labour Data Processor Onboarding
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Review data flows to contract labour agencies, supply chain partners, and logistics operators. Identify all processors handling employee and contractor personal data. Design DPA templates for manufacturing sector. Establish processor onboarding and periodic review process.

automation.label: Automation / Industry 4.0
automation.description: Technology companies, RPA implementers, AI/ML platforms, IoT device manufacturers, and Industry 4.0 solution providers processing personal data through automated systems, sensors, and AI decision-making.
sensitive_data_types: AI model training datasets containing personal data
Biometric data from computer vision and facial recognition systems
Behavioural data from IoT sensors and wearables
Automated decision-making output data (credit scores, performance ratings, risk classifications)
Digital twin data with worker activity inference
RPA bot audit logs containing personal data
Vehicle tracking and telematics data
applicable_regulations: SEBI guidelines on algorithmic trading (where applicable)
EU AI Act (for India-EU data flows)
automation.risk_notes: Automation and AI systems raise unique DPDP concerns: automated decisions affecting individuals (credit, employment, insurance) require disclosure under DPDP even if no specific right to explanation is yet mandated. Large-scale training datasets may contain personal data collected without adequate consent. IoT devices collecting ambient data may incidentally capture personal data without explicit collection moments.

additional_requirements.text: AI and ML model training datasets must be audited to confirm all personal data was collected with consent or legitimate use for the specific training purpose. Consent obtained for one purpose (e.g. service delivery) does not automatically extend to AI model training.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Training data provenance records; consent basis for each training dataset; data lineage documentation; evidence re-consent obtained where original consent did not cover AI training
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.8(1) + MeitY AI governance framework

additional_requirements.text: Where automated decision-making systems make or significantly influence decisions affecting data principals (employment screening, credit decisions, insurance pricing), a notice must inform data principals that automated processing is occurring and the logic involved.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Disclosure in privacy notice about automated decision-making; evidence notice precedes automated processing; process for human review of automated decisions upon request
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.5 + emerging DPDP rules on automated decisions

additional_requirements.text: AI model inversion and membership inference attacks can expose personal training data. Models trained on personal data must implement differential privacy, model access controls, and regular adversarial robustness testing.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: AI model security assessment; differential privacy implementation evidence; model access controls; adversarial testing records; data poisoning prevention measures

additional_requirements.text: RPA bots that process personal data must operate under explicit DPAs with the RPA platform vendor. Bot activity logs must not retain personal data beyond the immediate processing transaction unless separately justified.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: RPA platform DPA; bot personal data inventory; bot log retention policy; evidence personal data purged from bot logs after transaction completion

additional_requirements.text: IoT devices and sensors deployed in spaces where individuals are present must display notice of data collection, including device type, data collected, and purpose. Ambient data collection without notice is prohibited.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: IoT device notice labels or digital disclosures; IoT data inventory; personal data minimisation assessment for sensor data; evidence non-personal ambient data is not linked back to individuals

additional_requirements.text: AI systems processing biometric data (face recognition), health data, or making automated decisions at scale must undergo DPIA before deployment. Results must be reviewed by the DPO and documented.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: DPIA for qualifying AI systems; DPO sign-off on DPIA; evidence DPIA conducted before deployment; risk treatment decisions documented

intake_questions: What AI/ML models are in production that process personal data? List use cases (hiring, credit, insurance, fraud detection, recommendations).
How was the training data for each model collected, and was consent obtained specifically for AI training use?
Do you deploy facial recognition, computer vision, or biometric AI systems? Where are they deployed?
What RPA platforms are in use, and what personal data do bots process during automation workflows?
Do you manufacture IoT devices? If yes, what data do they collect and how is consent obtained from device users?
Have you conducted DPIAs for any AI systems? If yes, for which systems?

evidence_requests.title: AI training data provenance and consent basis records
evidence_requests.description: Training dataset inventory with consent basis documentation and data lineage records

evidence_requests.title: Automated decision-making disclosure in privacy notice
evidence_requests.description: Privacy notice sections covering automated processing; human review process documentation

evidence_requests.title: AI model security assessment and differential privacy evidence
evidence_requests.description: AI security assessment report; differential privacy implementation; model access control configuration

evidence_requests.title: DPIA records for AI systems
evidence_requests.description: Completed DPIAs for qualifying AI systems with DPO sign-off

advisory_clinic_templates.title: AI/ML Training Data Governance and Consent Remediation
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Audit all training datasets for personal data and consent coverage. Map existing consent to DPDP training data requirements. Design re-consent strategy for datasets where original consent did not cover AI training. Produce training data governance policy and lineage tracking process.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Automated Decision-Making Transparency and Human Review Design
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Inventory all automated decision systems affecting individuals. Design disclosure notices for each. Define human review trigger criteria and process. Produce explainability standard per model type (credit, HR, insurance). Align with emerging DPDP rules on automated decisions.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: AI DPIA Methodology and Deployment Governance
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Design DPIA methodology for AI systems covering data inputs, model outputs, affected individuals, and mitigations. Conduct DPIA for highest-risk system as a pilot. Establish AI deployment governance gate requiring DPIA completion. Produce DPIA template and DPO review checklist.

financial_services.label: Financial Services / Fintech
financial_services.description: Banks, NBFCs, insurance companies, payment aggregators, stock brokers, lending platforms, and fintech companies processing financial personal data of Indian customers. Subject to DPDP Act plus RBI, SEBI, IRDAI, and NPCI regulations.
sensitive_data_types: KYC data (Aadhaar, PAN, passport, voter ID)
Bank account and transaction history
Credit score and credit history
Insurance policy and claims data
Investment portfolio and trading history
Salary and income data
UPI transaction data
Loan application data (income, assets, liabilities)
Fraud detection behavioural data
applicable_regulations: RBI Master Direction on Digital Payment Security Controls
NPCI Operating Circulars on data handling
Prevention of Money Laundering Act (PMLA) — retention obligations
financial_services.risk_notes: Financial personal data is among the most sensitive under DPDP. KYC data involving Aadhaar is subject to additional UIDAI restrictions. Retention obligations under PMLA and tax laws may extend beyond DPDP's storage limitation principles, requiring documented justification. Credit scoring and automated lending decisions create automated decision-making disclosure obligations. Cross-border transfer restrictions intersect with RBI's data localisation mandate.

additional_requirements.text: KYC data collected under regulatory obligation (RBI guidelines) must be used only for identity verification and AML compliance. KYC data must not be used for credit scoring, marketing profiling, or cross-sold to affiliates without separate consent.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: KYC data use policy limiting use to regulatory purpose; technical controls preventing KYC data use in marketing systems; evidence Aadhaar data handled per UIDAI guidelines
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.8(1) + RBI KYC Master Direction + UIDAI guidelines

additional_requirements.text: Customers must be informed when automated credit decisions, insurance underwriting decisions, or fraud risk classifications are made about them using their personal data. Customers must have a mechanism to seek human review of adverse automated decisions.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Credit/underwriting decision disclosure in terms or notification; human review process for adverse decisions; audit log of automated decision triggers
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.5 + IRDAI AI guidelines

additional_requirements.text: Behavioural profiling of customers for cross-sell marketing, investment product recommendations, or third-party data sharing must be based on explicit separate consent, distinct from product terms of service.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Separate consent for marketing profiling; opt-in mechanism for cross-sell; evidence product access not conditioned on marketing consent; consent withdrawal does not affect core product

additional_requirements.text: RBI mandates data localisation for payment systems data. Financial personal data covered by RBI data localisation requirements must be stored in India. Cross-border transfers of this data are prohibited except for permitted limited mirroring.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Data localisation compliance assessment; payment data storage location evidence (India-based servers); cross-border transfer prohibition implementation; RBI audit reports
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.16 + RBI Data Localisation Directive

additional_requirements.text: Transaction data and financial history must be retained for the minimum period mandated by PMLA, Income Tax Act, and sector regulations. Retention beyond minimum must be justified and documented. Post-retention period, data must be deleted or anonymised.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Retention schedule by data type citing specific legal mandate; evidence of automated deletion after retention period; anonymisation process for analytical data post-retention
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.8(3) + PMLA 2002 + Income Tax Act 1961

additional_requirements.text: Financial institutions must designate a DPO or Privacy Officer aligned to both DPDP obligations and RBI/SEBI/IRDAI compliance requirements. The DPO must be involved in all new product data assessments.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: DPO or Privacy Officer appointment; DPO involvement in product privacy review process; dual DPDP-sector regulator compliance mandate in DPO charter
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.10 + SEBI Cybersecurity Framework

intake_questions: Do you process Aadhaar data for KYC? If yes, are you an AUA/KUA under UIDAI? What data minimisation controls are in place?
Do you use automated credit scoring or insurance underwriting models? How are affected customers informed?
Does your organisation share customer financial data with affiliates or third-party marketing platforms? On what legal basis?
Are all payment systems data stored in India per RBI data localisation requirements?
What is the retention schedule for transaction data, KYC records, and loan files, and which law mandates each retention period?
Do you operate a credit bureau, loan aggregator, or insurance comparison platform where personal data flows to multiple fiduciaries?

evidence_requests.title: KYC data use policy and UIDAI compliance evidence
evidence_requests.description: KYC data use restriction policy; UIDAI guidelines compliance evidence; technical controls preventing KYC data use beyond regulatory purpose

evidence_requests.title: Automated decision disclosure and human review process
evidence_requests.description: Customer disclosure for automated credit/underwriting decisions; human review SOP for adverse decisions

evidence_requests.title: RBI data localisation compliance evidence
evidence_requests.description: Data storage location confirmation for payment systems; RBI audit or self-assessment records

evidence_requests.title: Financial data retention schedule with legal citations
evidence_requests.description: Retention schedule citing PMLA, Income Tax, and sector law retention mandates per data type

advisory_clinic_templates.title: KYC Data Minimisation and Aadhaar Handling Workshop
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Review KYC data collection scope across onboarding flows. Assess Aadhaar handling against UIDAI guidelines. Identify over-collection and downstream secondary use. Design KYC data minimisation plan and technical controls for purpose limitation. Produce UIDAI compliance checklist.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Credit Scoring and Automated Underwriting Explainability Design
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Review automated decision systems (credit scoring, fraud, insurance). Design customer-facing disclosure for each decision type. Define human review trigger criteria and SLA. Assess alignment with RBI and IRDAI AI guidance. Produce explainability brief per model.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: RBI Data Localisation and DPDP Cross-Border Transfer Programme
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Map all financial personal data flows including cloud providers, card networks, and offshore processors. Identify RBI localisation conflicts with current architecture. Design compliant cross-border transfer controls and India-resident storage architecture for regulated data.

education.label: Education / EdTech
education.description: Schools, colleges, universities, EdTech platforms, online learning providers, and coaching institutes processing student personal data. DPDP Section 9 children's provisions apply to all students under 18.
sensitive_data_types: Student academic performance data
Biometric data (fingerprint attendance, face recognition in classrooms)
Online learning behaviour and engagement data
Assessment and examination results
Student health and disability records
Financial aid and scholarship data
Parental contact and family income data
Student geolocation and campus access data
Counselling and mental health records
applicable_regulations: DPDP Act 2023 (Section 9 — children's data is critical)
Central Board of Secondary Education (CBSE) guidelines
Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act (POCSO) — data handling provisions
education.risk_notes: DPDP Section 9 requires verifiable parental consent for all processing of personal data of children under 18. The vast majority of school students and many college students fall in this category. EdTech platforms often collect extensive behavioural data (click streams, attention monitoring, video recordings) that goes far beyond what students or parents expect. Children's data must not be used for profiling, behavioural advertising, or algorithmic targeting.

additional_requirements.text: For all students under 18, the educational institution must obtain verifiable consent from a parent or guardian before enrolling the student on any digital platform and before processing personal data beyond basic administrative records.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Parental consent forms for digital platform enrolment; age verification mechanism; consent for each platform or data system the student uses; evidence consent is verifiable not self-certified

additional_requirements.text: Student behavioural data collected via EdTech platforms (clickstream, attention monitoring, keystroke dynamics) must be limited to stated educational purposes. This data must not be used for advertising, profiling, or sold to third parties.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: EdTech platform data use policy; evidence behavioural data excluded from advertising or profiling; contracts with EdTech vendors prohibiting secondary use

additional_requirements.text: All third-party EdTech platforms, assessment tools, and Learning Management Systems (LMS) used by the institution must have signed DPAs that comply with DPDP obligations and explicitly prohibit children's data profiling.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: DPAs with all EdTech vendors, LMS providers, and assessment platforms; children's data prohibition clause; vendor security assessment records

additional_requirements.text: Student academic records, examination data, and counselling records must be protected with access controls ensuring only authorised faculty and administrative staff can access individual student records.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Student information system RBAC configuration; access logs; evidence counselling records have restricted access; pentest or security assessment of student data systems

additional_requirements.text: Scholarship, financial aid, and family income data collected for fee concession must be used only for the stated purpose and not shared with other departments, analytics platforms, or third parties without consent.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Financial aid data use policy; evidence of access restriction to admissions or finance office; no sharing with analytics or marketing platforms

additional_requirements.text: Students (or parents for minors) must be able to access, correct, and request erasure of academic records. Records must be transferable to other institutions upon legitimate request.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Student data access request process; academic record portability procedure; SLA for record transfer requests; erasure procedure for alumni records after legal retention period

intake_questions: What proportion of students enrolled are under 18? How is age verified and parental consent obtained for digital platform use?
Which third-party EdTech platforms, LMS, and assessment tools are deployed? Do DPAs exist with all vendors?
Do any platforms collect behavioural monitoring data (attention tracking, keystroke, clickstream)? What is the data use policy?
Do you collect family income or scholarship eligibility data? How is it protected and for how long is it retained?
Are online classes or examination sessions recorded? What is the consent and retention policy for recordings?
Do you share student data with alumni networks, analytics companies, or government portals?

evidence_requests.title: Parental consent forms and age verification mechanism
evidence_requests.description: Sample guardian consent forms for digital platform enrolment and age verification system documentation

evidence_requests.title: EdTech platform data use policy and profiling prohibition
evidence_requests.description: EdTech platform contracts and DPAs showing prohibition on student data profiling and advertising

evidence_requests.title: DPAs with all EdTech and LMS vendors
evidence_requests.description: Signed DPAs with all third-party education platforms and assessment tools

evidence_requests.title: Student information system access controls
evidence_requests.description: SIS RBAC configuration, access logs, and restricted access evidence for sensitive records (counselling, health)

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Parental Consent Management for Minor Student Data
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Design verifiable parental consent architecture for all digital platforms used by under-18 students. Review existing consent forms for adequacy under DPDP Section 9. Design digital consent management portal for parents. Map consent requirements per platform category (LMS, assessment, communication, EdTech).

advisory_clinic_templates.title: EdTech Vendor DPA Review and Privacy Standards
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Review all EdTech platform DPAs for DPDP compliance. Identify vendors lacking DPAs or with inadequate children's data provisions. Design standard DPA template for educational institutions. Establish vendor onboarding privacy checklist and annual review process.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Student Behavioural Data Use Policy and Data Minimisation
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Audit EdTech platforms for behavioural data collection (attention monitoring, keystroke, engagement analytics). Assess against Section 9(3) prohibited processing criteria. Design data minimisation policy and platform configuration standards. Produce student data use charter for parent communication.

ecommerce.label: E-Commerce / Retail
ecommerce.description: Online marketplaces, retail platforms, D2C brands, quick commerce, and subscription commerce processing customer purchase data, behavioural profiles, and payment information.
sensitive_data_types: Purchase history and browsing behaviour
Payment card data and UPI handles
Customer profiling and recommendation engine data
Loyalty programme purchase and redemption data
Delivery address and location history
Return and refund patterns
Third-party analytics and tracking data (cookies, pixel data)
Customer service interaction records
Wishlist and cart abandonment data
applicable_regulations: NPCI UPI Operating Circular on data
Bureau of Indian Standards — e-commerce data quality
Information Technology (Intermediaries Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules 2021
ecommerce.risk_notes: E-commerce platforms extensively profile customers for personalisation and targeted advertising. Third-party analytics tools (Google Analytics, Meta Pixel, etc.) may constitute data processor relationships requiring DPAs. Cookie-based tracking requires explicit consent mechanisms. Loyalty programme data creates long-term personal data retention obligations that must be balanced against DPDP storage limitation. Payment tokenisation under RBI guidelines must be maintained alongside DPDP security obligations.

additional_requirements.text: Behavioural profiling for personalised product recommendations, dynamic pricing, and targeted advertising must be based on explicit consent separate from the purchase terms of service. Users must be able to opt out of profiling without affecting their ability to make purchases.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Separate consent for behavioural profiling; opt-out mechanism for personalisation; evidence purchase service not conditioned on profiling consent; profiling opt-out test

additional_requirements.text: Third-party analytics tools, advertising pixels, and tracking SDKs embedded in the platform must be covered by a cookie consent mechanism that defaults to non-tracking and requires explicit user opt-in.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Cookie consent banner defaulting to opt-in model; list of all tracking pixels and SDKs with purpose; evidence non-consent blocks third-party tracking; DPAs with analytics vendors

additional_requirements.text: Payment aggregators, logistics partners, and fulfilment warehouses that process customer personal data on behalf of the e-commerce platform must have signed DPAs covering DPDP security and purpose limitation obligations.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: DPAs with payment aggregators, logistics partners, and fulfilment operators; vendor DPA inventory; evidence all third parties processing customer data have signed DPAs

additional_requirements.text: Loyalty programme data (purchase history, tier data, redemption behaviour) must be used only for loyalty benefit administration and not sold or shared with brand partners without explicit separate customer consent.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Loyalty programme data use policy; evidence loyalty data not shared with brand partners without consent; opt-in mechanism for partner data sharing

additional_requirements.text: Payment card and UPI handle data must be tokenised per RBI tokenisation guidelines. Full card numbers must not be stored. Customer payment data must be protected by PCI-DSS compliant controls.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: RBI tokenisation compliance evidence; no full card number storage confirmation; PCI-DSS compliance report or SAQ; UPI handle data encryption evidence
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.8(5) + RBI Tokenisation Circular

additional_requirements.text: Customers must be able to access their full purchase history, profile data, and behavioural data collected by the platform, and request erasure upon account deletion. Account deletion must trigger data deletion across recommendation and analytics systems.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Account data download feature (DSAR); account deletion procedure with data deletion scope; evidence deletion propagates to analytics and recommendation systems; SLA for account deletion completion

intake_questions: What third-party analytics tools, advertising pixels, and tracking SDKs are embedded in your platform?
Do you have a cookie consent mechanism? Does it default to opt-in or opt-out for non-essential tracking?
Do you profile customers for personalised recommendations or dynamic pricing? Is this covered by separate consent?
Do you share customer purchase or behavioural data with brand partners or advertisers? On what legal basis?
What is the account deletion procedure? Does it cascade to all analytics and recommendation systems?
Are all payment aggregators and logistics partners covered by DPAs?

evidence_requests.title: Behavioural profiling consent mechanism
evidence_requests.description: Separate consent UI for profiling; opt-out test evidence; profiling consent records

evidence_requests.title: Cookie consent banner configuration and tracker inventory
evidence_requests.description: Cookie consent tool configuration; full list of tracking pixels and SDKs deployed; DPAs with analytics vendors

evidence_requests.title: Payment tokenisation and PCI-DSS compliance evidence
evidence_requests.description: RBI tokenisation compliance documentation; PCI-DSS report or SAQ; confirmation of no full card storage

evidence_requests.title: Account deletion procedure and DSAR capability
evidence_requests.description: Account deletion workflow showing data scope; DSAR data download feature; deletion propagation evidence

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Cookie Consent and Behavioural Profiling Architecture Review
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Audit all tracking technologies deployed (pixels, SDKs, cookies, server-side events). Review consent mechanism against DPDP requirements. Redesign consent banner for DPDP opt-in model. Map each tracker to a purpose and consent basis. Produce tracker inventory and consent mapping document.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Loyalty Programme and Customer Profiling Privacy Design
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Review loyalty programme data flows, partner sharing arrangements, and profiling depth. Assess against DPDP purpose limitation. Design separate consent architecture for partner sharing. Produce loyalty data governance policy and partner DPA template with data use restrictions.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Customer Data Portability and Account Deletion Implementation
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Design end-to-end account deletion workflow covering all customer data across recommendation engines, analytics, loyalty, and payment systems. Build DSAR response capability for customer data download. Define deletion SLA and verify cascade deletion to all processors.

telecom.label: Telecom
telecom.description: Mobile network operators, ISPs, OTT communication platforms, and MVNO operators processing subscriber data, location data, call detail records, and communication content metadata.
sensitive_data_types: Subscriber identity (name, address, Aadhaar for CAF)
Mobile number and IMEI/IMSI data
Call Detail Records (CDR) and SMS records
Real-time and historical location data (tower triangulation, GPS)
Internet browsing history and DNS queries
OTT messaging metadata
Payment recharge and billing data
Roaming data and international CDRs
Device fingerprint data
applicable_regulations: Telecom Regulatory Authority of India (TRAI) regulations
Department of Telecommunications (DoT) licencing conditions on data
TRAI Consultation Paper on Data Privacy 2018
telecom.risk_notes: Location data in telecom is among the most sensitive personal data as it can reveal intimate details of daily life, movements, religious practices, and political affiliations. CDRs can expose social networks and communication patterns. Telecoms face tension between DPDP privacy obligations and lawful interception obligations from intelligence agencies. Roaming data sharing with foreign operators requires careful DPDP cross-border transfer assessment.

additional_requirements.text: Real-time location data may only be used for network operations and emergency services. Commercial use of location data for advertising, analytics, or third-party sharing requires explicit separate subscriber consent and must be easy to withdraw.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Location data use policy limiting primary use to network operations; explicit opt-in consent for commercial location use; subscriber opt-out mechanism; evidence location data not sold to third parties without consent

additional_requirements.text: Call Detail Records (CDR) and subscriber data must be protected with encryption at rest and strict access controls. CDR access must be logged, and access restricted to authorised network operations, billing, and lawful interception teams.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: CDR database encryption configuration; CDR access control and RBAC records; CDR access audit logs; evidence CDR not accessible to marketing or analytics teams without anonymisation

additional_requirements.text: CDR and browsing history data retained for lawful interception or regulatory compliance purposes must not be used for commercial purposes such as profiling, targeted advertising, or market analytics without de-identification and subscriber consent.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Lawful interception data silo architecture; evidence CDR data not cross-used for commercial analytics; de-identification process for analytics use cases; subscriber consent for commercial use of network data
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.8(1) + DoT licencing conditions

additional_requirements.text: Subscriber data and CDRs shared with foreign operators for international roaming must be governed by DPAs. Only data strictly necessary for roaming service delivery may be transferred. Transfer to non-approved countries requires additional safeguards.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Roaming DPAs with foreign operators; data minimisation for roaming data transfers; country compliance assessment for roaming partners; cross-border transfer log
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.16 + Unified Licence Agreement

additional_requirements.text: A breach affecting CDR, location data, or subscriber identity must be treated as critical given the sensitivity of communication metadata. DPBI notification must be triggered immediately and subscribers notified with specific detail of affected data.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Telecom breach classification in IRP; CDR/location breach escalation procedure; subscriber notification capability at scale; DPBI notification drill record specific to telecom breach scenario

intake_questions: Does the organisation share location data with any third parties? For what purpose and on what legal basis?
How are CDRs protected and who has access? Are CDR access logs maintained?
Is subscriber data used for targeted advertising or profiling? How is consent obtained for commercial data use?
What roaming data is shared with foreign operators? Are DPAs in place with all international roaming partners?
How does the organisation manage tension between DPDP privacy obligations and lawful interception requirements?
What is the retention period for CDRs, location data, and browsing history, and which law mandates each?

evidence_requests.title: Location data consent mechanism and commercial use controls
evidence_requests.description: Subscriber consent UI for location data commercial use; opt-out mechanism; location data use policy

evidence_requests.title: CDR encryption and access control configuration
evidence_requests.description: CDR database encryption evidence; RBAC configuration; CDR access audit logs

evidence_requests.title: Roaming DPAs with foreign operators
evidence_requests.description: DPAs with international roaming partners; data minimisation evidence for roaming transfers

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Location Data Privacy and Commercial Use Consent Framework
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Map all location data flows (real-time, historical, aggregated). Assess current commercial use against DPDP consent requirements. Design explicit opt-in mechanism for commercial location use. Define network-operations-only baseline that does not require consent. Produce location data governance policy.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: CDR Security Architecture and Access Control Review
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Review CDR storage architecture, encryption at rest and in transit, access controls, and audit logging. Assess segregation between network operations, billing, and analytics. Design CDR access governance framework. Recommend anonymisation approach for analytics use cases.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: International Roaming Data Transfer Compliance Programme
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Map roaming data flows to all international partner networks. Assess partner country DPDP transfer compliance. Design standard DPA template for roaming agreements. Establish data minimisation controls for roaming data. Produce cross-border transfer assessment for top 10 roaming partners.

government.label: Government / Public Sector
government.description: Central and state government ministries, departments, PSUs, municipalities, and government-owned platforms processing citizen personal data for public services, welfare schemes, and law enforcement.
sensitive_data_types: Citizen identity data (Aadhaar, voter ID, driving licence, passport)
Beneficiary data for welfare schemes (income, caste, family composition)
Tax records and financial declarations
Health data from government hospitals and health schemes
Land and property records
Criminal record and law enforcement data
Biometric data from e-KYC and DigiLocker integrations
Student data from government education platforms
Census and survey response data
applicable_regulations: DPDP Act 2023 — Note: Government entities may qualify for exemptions under Section 17
government.risk_notes: Government entities may be exempt from certain DPDP provisions under Section 17 where processing is in the interest of sovereignty, security, public order, or prevention of offences. However, Section 17 exemptions are narrow and do not exempt all government processing. Welfare scheme data involves highly sensitive beneficiary information at scale. Aadhaar-based authentication falls under UIDAI's own regulatory framework in addition to DPDP.

additional_requirements.text: Government entities using legitimate use as the basis for processing citizen data must document the specific statutory function or public interest that justifies the processing. Blanket claims of government authority are insufficient.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Statutory mandate or policy order authorising each processing activity; legal basis register for all citizen data processing; evidence processing is proportionate to statutory function
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.7 + relevant enabling legislation

additional_requirements.text: Beneficiary data collected for welfare schemes (PM-KISAN, Ayushman Bharat, MGNREGS, etc.) must be used only for the specific scheme benefit delivery. Cross-scheme data sharing or use for non-welfare purposes requires documented justification.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Welfare scheme data use policy; technical controls preventing cross-scheme data sharing without authorisation; DPDPA-compliant data sharing protocols between ministries

additional_requirements.text: Citizen personal data stored on government platforms must meet NIC and CERT-In security guidelines. All e-governance platforms handling sensitive citizen data must undergo annual security audit and VAPT.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: NIC security compliance assessment; CERT-In audit reports; annual VAPT report for citizen-facing platforms; encryption configuration for citizen data at rest and in transit
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.8(5) + MeitY security guidelines + CERT-In directives

additional_requirements.text: Where Section 17 exemptions are claimed, the specific exemption and its scope must be documented. Exemptions do not extend beyond what is strictly necessary, and even exempt processing must implement reasonable security safeguards.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Section 17 exemption claim documentation with legal justification; evidence exemption scope is precisely defined; security safeguards implemented despite exemption claim

additional_requirements.text: Citizens must be able to access and correct their personal data held by government databases including tax records, property records, and health scheme data. The grievance redressal mechanism must be publicly accessible and responsive.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Online grievance portal for personal data access; SLA for government data access requests; correction process for Aadhaar-linked government records; sample fulfilled citizen data requests
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.11-13 (where Section 17 exemption does not apply)

additional_requirements.text: IT vendors and cloud service providers contracted to build or operate government platforms processing citizen data must be bound by DPAs. Vendor access to citizen data must be limited to technical support functions and logged.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: DPAs with all government IT vendors and cloud providers; vendor access control records; audit logs of vendor access to citizen data; data residency clause in government cloud contracts

intake_questions: Which DPDP Act Section 17 exemptions does the entity claim, and for which specific processing activities?
What welfare scheme beneficiary data is processed? Is cross-scheme data sharing authorised and documented?
Has each citizen-facing e-governance platform undergone VAPT in the last 12 months?
Are all IT vendors and cloud providers contracted with DPAs that restrict citizen data use to authorised purposes?
How do citizens currently access and correct their personal data held by the department?
Does the entity process Aadhaar data? If yes, is the entity registered as AUA/KUA with UIDAI?

evidence_requests.title: Statutory mandate and legal basis register for citizen data processing
evidence_requests.description: Legal basis register citing specific statutory authority for each citizen data processing activity

evidence_requests.title: Welfare scheme data use policy and cross-scheme controls
evidence_requests.description: Scheme data use policy and technical controls preventing unauthorised cross-scheme data sharing

evidence_requests.title: NIC/CERT-In compliance and VAPT reports for e-governance platforms
evidence_requests.description: CERT-In audit reports and annual VAPT results for all citizen-facing government platforms

evidence_requests.title: Section 17 exemption documentation
evidence_requests.description: Documented Section 17 claims with legal justification and scope definition per processing activity

evidence_requests.title: IT vendor DPAs for government platforms
evidence_requests.description: DPAs with all IT vendors and cloud providers; data residency clauses; vendor access logs

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Section 17 Exemption Mapping and Scope Documentation
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Map all government processing activities against Section 17 exemption categories. Identify activities that cannot be exempted and require full DPDP compliance. Produce exemption scope documentation for each claimed exemption with legal justification. Design residual compliance programme for non-exempt processing.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Welfare Scheme Data Governance and Cross-Ministry Sharing Controls
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Map all welfare scheme personal data flows including cross-scheme data sharing. Assess against DPDP purpose limitation and statutory authority. Design data sharing protocol for inter-ministry data flows. Produce data sharing MOU template with DPDP obligations. Define citizen notification for data sharing.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: E-Governance Platform VAPT and Security Compliance
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Review NIC and CERT-In security compliance status for all citizen-facing platforms. Conduct VAPT scoping for highest-risk platforms. Prioritise remediation by citizen data sensitivity and platform scale. Produce security improvement roadmap aligned to MeitY guidelines and DPDP obligations.

pharma.label: Pharma / Life Sciences
pharma.description: Pharmaceutical companies, Contract Research Organisations (CROs), clinical trial sponsors, medical device manufacturers, and life sciences organisations processing clinical trial participant data, adverse event records, and prescription data. Clinical trial participant consent and CDSCO data sharing obligations intersect directly with DPDP.
sensitive_data_types: Clinical trial participant data (demographics, medical history, outcomes)
Adverse event and pharmacovigilance reports
Prescription and dispensing records
Employee health and occupational medical records
Biometric access logs for GMP manufacturing areas
Regulatory submission data (CDSCO/DCGI filings)
Genetic and biomarker data from trials
CRO sub-investigator personal data
applicable_regulations: CDSCO (Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation) Good Clinical Practice guidelines
ICMR National Ethical Guidelines for Biomedical and Health Research 2017
DCGI New Drugs and Clinical Trials Rules 2019
pharma.risk_notes: AMBER: Clinical trial participants are a vulnerable data principal population where consent must be genuinely free and informed, not conditioned on trial participation benefits. Adverse event data shared with CDSCO under pharmacovigilance obligations may conflict with DPDP data minimisation — document the regulatory necessity for each data element disclosed. CROs acting as data processors require robust DPAs specifying purpose limitation for participant data.

additional_requirements.text: Clinical trial participants must provide granular informed consent covering: data collection, processing by the sponsor, sharing with CDSCO/regulatory authorities, transfer to CROs, and any secondary research use. Consent must be obtained before enrolment and must be re-sought if data use purpose changes materially.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Informed Consent Forms (ICFs) with granular consent per purpose; IRB/IEC approval of ICF; evidence ICF presented before enrolment; process for re-consent on material purpose change; multilingual ICF where trial sites are in non-English-speaking regions
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.6 + Schedule Y Rule 122DAB + ICMR guidelines

additional_requirements.text: CROs and sub-investigators processing participant data on behalf of the sponsor must be bound by written DPAs covering DPDP obligations, purpose limitation to clinical trial activities, breach notification, and data return or deletion upon trial completion.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Signed DPAs with all CROs and site management organisations; DPA purpose limitation clause restricted to clinical trial; data return/deletion clause at trial end; evidence DPAs predate participant data transfer
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.8(8) + CDSCO GCP guidelines

additional_requirements.text: Transfer of clinical trial participant data to foreign regulatory authorities (FDA, EMA), international sponsors, or global safety databases must be assessed for DPDP cross-border transfer compliance. Transfers to countries without Central Government approval require additional safeguards.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Cross-border transfer inventory for participant data; DPAs with foreign sponsors and regulatory data repositories; country compliance assessment; Standard Contractual Clauses or equivalent safeguards for each destination
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.16 + CDSCO New Drugs and Clinical Trials Rules 2019

additional_requirements.text: Pharmacovigilance and adverse event data submitted to CDSCO/PvPI must use the minimum participant identifiers necessary to fulfil reporting obligations. Full participant identity must not be included in regulatory submissions unless specifically required by CDSCO.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Adverse event report templates showing pseudonymised participant identifiers; pharmacovigilance data minimisation policy; evidence full identity excluded from CIOMS/E2B reports unless mandated; PvPI submission audit trail
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.8(1) + PvPI guidelines + ICH E2B guidelines

additional_requirements.text: AI-based drug discovery platforms and digital biomarker tools that process participant health and genetic data at scale must undergo DPIA before deployment. Outcomes must be reviewed by the DPO and documented.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: DPIA for AI drug discovery platforms; DPO sign-off; evidence DPIA conducted before platform deployment; risk treatment decisions with accepted residual risks documented
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.10(2)(a) + ICMR AI in healthcare guidelines

additional_requirements.text: Trial participants retain the right to withdraw consent and request erasure of their personal data, subject to overriding regulatory obligations to retain safety data. Where full erasure is not possible due to regulatory mandate, participants must be informed and data pseudonymised.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Participant withdrawal and data erasure procedure; process for pseudonymisation where full erasure conflicts with regulatory retention; participant communication template for partial erasure; IRB-approved withdrawal handling policy
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.12 + Schedule Y + ICMR guidelines

additional_requirements.text: Clinical trial data management systems (CTMS, EDC, eTMF) must implement role-based access control, audit trails for all data modifications, and encryption for participant data at rest and in transit. Access must be restricted to study-specific personnel only.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: CTMS/EDC RBAC configuration; 21 CFR Part 11 / GCP-compliant audit trail evidence; encryption configuration; access restricted to study team roster; annual security assessment of CTMS and EDC platforms
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.8(5) + 21 CFR Part 11 + CDSCO GCP

intake_questions: What clinical trial phases are conducted in India and which EDC/CTMS systems are used to manage participant data?
Do you transfer participant data to foreign regulatory authorities (FDA, EMA) or international sponsor offices? Are DPDP cross-border transfer safeguards in place?
Are all CROs and sub-investigator sites bound by DPAs covering DPDP obligations?
How is adverse event pharmacovigilance data submitted to CDSCO — are participant identifiers pseudonymised in submissions?
Does your organisation use AI or machine learning for drug discovery or digital biomarker analysis on participant data? Have DPIAs been conducted?
What is the process when a trial participant withdraws consent — how is their data handled when regulatory retention obligations apply?

evidence_requests.title: Informed Consent Forms (ICFs) with granular purpose consent
evidence_requests.description: Sample ICFs from active trials showing separate consent for treatment, regulatory reporting, CRO sharing, and secondary research; IRB/IEC approval records

evidence_requests.title: CRO and sub-investigator DPAs
evidence_requests.description: Signed DPAs with all CROs, site management organisations, and sub-investigator sites; DPA purpose limitation and data return clauses
