evidence_requests.title: CTMS and EDC security configuration and audit trails
evidence_requests.description: RBAC configuration for CTMS/EDC; audit trail sample; encryption configuration; annual security assessment report

evidence_requests.title: Cross-border participant data transfer inventory and safeguards
evidence_requests.description: Inventory of all international participant data transfers; country compliance assessment; DPAs or SCCs with foreign sponsors and regulatory repositories

evidence_requests.title: Participant withdrawal and pseudonymisation procedure
evidence_requests.description: Documented process for handling withdrawal requests including pseudonymisation where regulatory retention prevents full erasure; participant communication templates

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Clinical Trial Informed Consent Architecture Workshop
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Review existing ICFs across active and planned trials for DPDP granularity. Map consent to DPDP Section 6 requirements and ICMR guidelines. Identify bundled or insufficient consents. Design granular, purpose-specific ICF templates. Produce ICF design standard and review checklist for future trials.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: CRO and Investigator Site DPDP DPA Programme
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Review all existing CRO and sub-investigator data processing agreements for DPDP compliance gaps. Design DPDP-compliant DPA template for clinical trial contexts. Establish CRO onboarding privacy checklist and annual DPA review cycle. Produce DPA amendment schedule for non-compliant existing agreements.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Pharmacovigilance Data Minimisation and CDSCO Reporting Workshop
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Review adverse event and SAE reporting workflows for participant identifier minimisation. Assess CIOMS/E2B report templates against DPDP data minimisation obligations. Design pseudonymisation approach for regulatory submissions. Produce pharmacovigilance data governance policy.

media.label: Media / Entertainment / OTT
media.description: OTT streaming platforms, digital media companies, news portals, gaming platforms, and entertainment content providers processing viewer profiles, watch history, and behavioural targeting data. DPDP Section 9 children's provisions apply to platforms accessible to minors.
sensitive_data_types: Viewer profiles and demographic data
Watch history and content engagement data
Subscription and payment data
Behavioural targeting and recommendation algorithm data
Minors' content access logs and parental control settings
Social login and linked account data
Location data for geo-restricted content delivery
Advertising audience segment data
applicable_regulations: DPDP Act 2023 (Section 9 — minors on OTT platforms)
Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules 2021
Advertising Standards Council of India (ASCI) guidelines on digital advertising
media.risk_notes: AMBER: OTT and media platforms engage in extensive behavioural profiling for content recommendations and targeted advertising. DPDP Section 9 requires verifiable parental consent before any processing of data for users under 18 — age gating and parental consent mechanisms must be robust, not self-certified. Cross-border content licensing data transfers (to international studios and distributors) require DPDP cross-border transfer compliance. Targeted advertising consent must be opt-in and separable from content access.

additional_requirements.text: OTT platforms must implement age verification mechanisms that go beyond self-declaration to verify that users are 18 or above before processing their data without parental consent. For identified or suspected minors, verifiable parental consent must be obtained before enrolment and before any profiling.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Age verification mechanism documentation (not self-declaration alone); parental consent flow for under-18 users; evidence profiling disabled for minors without parental consent; DPDP S.9 compliance assessment

additional_requirements.text: Behavioural profiling for content recommendations, targeted advertising, and audience segmentation must be based on explicit separate consent distinct from subscription terms. Users must be able to access the platform and basic content without consenting to profiling.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Separate consent for behavioural profiling and advertising; opt-in mechanism not bundled with subscription; evidence content access not conditioned on profiling consent; consent withdrawal test showing profiling ceases

additional_requirements.text: Viewer data and content licensing analytics shared with international studios, content owners, or licensing partners under cross-border content agreements must comply with DPDP cross-border transfer requirements. DPAs must be in place with each international recipient.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Inventory of cross-border viewer data transfers to studios and licensing partners; DPAs with all international data recipients; country compliance assessment; data minimisation for cross-border content analytics

additional_requirements.text: Watch history, content engagement data, and viewer profiles must not be sold to third-party advertisers or data brokers without explicit viewer consent. Aggregated, anonymised audience data may be shared with advertisers provided it cannot be re-identified.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Data sharing policy for advertisers; evidence individual viewer data not sold without consent; de-identification methodology for audience analytics shared with advertisers; audit of third-party advertiser data access
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.8(1) + ASCI digital advertising guidelines

additional_requirements.text: Viewers must be able to access, correct, and request erasure of their profile, watch history, and recommendation data. Account deletion must cascade to advertising audience segments, recommendation engine models trained on their data, and all analytics processors.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: User data download (DSAR) capability; account deletion workflow with data scope confirmation; evidence deletion propagates to recommendation system and advertising segments; SLA for account deletion completion

additional_requirements.text: Third-party advertising technology vendors, CDN providers, and analytics platforms processing viewer data on behalf of the media platform must be bound by signed DPAs covering DPDP obligations and prohibiting secondary use of viewer data.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: DPAs with all ad-tech vendors, analytics platforms, and CDN providers; prohibition on secondary use clause; vendor inventory; evidence all sub-processors are contractually bound

intake_questions: Does your platform have users under 18? How is age verified and how is parental consent obtained for minor users?
What behavioural profiling is conducted for content recommendations and targeted advertising? Is separate consent obtained for profiling?
Do you share viewer data with international studios, distributors, or content licensors? Are DPDP cross-border transfer safeguards in place?
What ad-tech vendors, analytics SDKs, and CDN providers have access to viewer data? Are DPAs in place with all of them?
What is the account deletion procedure, and does it cascade to recommendation engine training data and advertising audience segments?
Does your platform serve targeted advertising? Is consent for advertising profiling opt-in and separable from content access?

evidence_requests.title: Age verification mechanism and parental consent flow for minors
evidence_requests.description: Age verification system documentation; parental consent UI screenshots; evidence profiling disabled for minor users without parental consent

evidence_requests.title: Behavioural profiling consent mechanism
evidence_requests.description: Separate consent UI for profiling and advertising; opt-in not bundled with subscription; consent withdrawal test showing profiling cessation

evidence_requests.title: Ad-tech and analytics vendor DPAs
evidence_requests.description: Signed DPAs with all advertising technology vendors, analytics platforms, and CDN providers; secondary use prohibition clauses

evidence_requests.title: Account deletion and DSAR capability
evidence_requests.description: Account deletion workflow with data scope; DSAR data download feature; evidence deletion propagates to recommendation and advertising systems

advisory_clinic_templates.title: OTT Minor User Consent and Age Verification Design
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Review current age verification and parental consent mechanisms against DPDP Section 9 requirements. Design robust age gating beyond self-declaration. Produce parental consent architecture for minor profiles. Define prohibited processing for minor users and platform configuration standards.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Behavioural Profiling Consent and Ad-Tech Governance
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Audit all behavioural data collection points and ad-tech integrations. Review consent mechanism for profiling separability from subscription. Design DPDP-compliant consent architecture for targeted advertising. Produce ad-tech vendor DPA review checklist and standard DPA template.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Cross-Border Content Licensing Data Transfer Programme
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Map all viewer data flows to international studios, distributors, and licensing partners. Assess DPDP cross-border transfer compliance for each destination. Design DPA template for international content licensing data agreements. Produce cross-border transfer assessment for top 10 data destinations.

hospitality.label: Hospitality / Travel
hospitality.description: Hotels, airlines, travel agencies, online travel aggregators (OTAs), and hospitality chains processing guest identity documents, travel itineraries, payment data, and loyalty programme information. Mandatory police reporting obligations coexist with DPDP data minimisation principles.
sensitive_data_types: Passport and national ID copies (mandatory check-in)
Travel itineraries and booking history
Payment card and UPI data
Loyalty programme profiles and stay history
Biometric check-in data (face recognition at hotels and airports)
Health declarations and vaccination status (post-COVID)
Foreign guest registration data (FRRO reporting)
Special service requests (dietary, disability, medical)
applicable_regulations: Foreigners Act 1946 and Foreigners (Registration) Order 1992 (3-year guest record retention for foreign guests)
Foreigners Regional Registration Offices (FRRO) reporting requirements
Ministry of Home Affairs (MHA) C-Form reporting for foreign guest data
DGCA Civil Aviation Requirements (CAR) for passenger data
Tourism Act and Ministry of Tourism guidelines
Prevention of Money Laundering Act (PMLA) for high-value hospitality transactions
hospitality.risk_notes: AMBER: Hospitality operators face a direct tension between mandatory police and FRRO reporting obligations (requiring guest identity data retention for up to 3 years) and DPDP data minimisation and storage limitation principles. This conflict must be documented: retain what the law requires, delete what it does not. Loyalty programme data profiling creates long-term retention obligations requiring separate consent. Cross-border hotel chain transfers of guest data to international parent companies require DPDP cross-border transfer compliance.

additional_requirements.text: Guest identity data (passport, Aadhaar, voter ID) collected at check-in for mandatory MHA/FRRO reporting must be retained only for the legally mandated period (3 years for foreign guests under Foreigners Order) and deleted thereafter. Data must not be used for loyalty profiling, marketing, or shared with affiliates beyond the check-in compliance purpose.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Guest data retention schedule citing specific legal mandate (Foreigners Act, FRRO, hotel police reporting obligations); evidence identity data deleted after retention period; technical controls preventing identity data use for marketing; deletion audit logs
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.8(3) + Foreigners (Registration) Order 1992 + MHA C-Form requirements

additional_requirements.text: Loyalty programme enrolment must be based on explicit consent separate from the room booking. Profiling of guest stay history, preferences, and spend patterns for targeted marketing must be opt-in. Guests must be able to withdraw loyalty profiling consent without losing accumulated loyalty points.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Loyalty programme consent mechanism separate from booking; profiling opt-in evidence; opt-out of profiling not penalising loyalty balance; consent records for loyalty members

additional_requirements.text: International hotel chains and OTAs transferring guest data to parent companies or global reservation systems abroad must comply with DPDP cross-border transfer requirements. DPAs must be in place with the foreign parent entity and any global CRS (Central Reservation System) provider.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Cross-border transfer inventory for guest data; DPAs with international parent company and global CRS providers; country compliance assessment for each destination; data minimisation for international transfers

additional_requirements.text: Biometric check-in systems (face recognition at hotels and airports) must obtain explicit separate consent from guests before enrolment. Biometric check-in must be offered as an optional service, with non-biometric check-in always available as an alternative.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Biometric check-in consent form or digital consent mechanism; evidence non-biometric alternative available; biometric data deletion on checkout or upon request; biometric system vendor DPA

additional_requirements.text: OTA platforms, travel management companies, and booking aggregators processing hotel guest data on behalf of properties must be bound by DPAs. The hotel or airline remains accountable as data fiduciary for guest personal data processed by OTA platforms.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: DPAs with all OTA platforms, travel agencies, and booking aggregators; data processing scope limitation in DPAs; evidence hotels remain accountable for guest data processed by OTAs

additional_requirements.text: Payment card data processed at hotel properties and airline booking systems must comply with PCI-DSS requirements and RBI tokenisation guidelines. Guest payment data must not be retained beyond immediate transaction completion unless tokenised.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: PCI-DSS compliance report or SAQ; RBI tokenisation compliance for UPI/card data; evidence full card numbers not retained; payment system security assessment
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.8(5) + RBI Tokenisation Circular + PCI-DSS

intake_questions: How does the property comply with MHA/FRRO C-Form reporting? What guest data is collected and for how long is it retained?
Does the property offer biometric check-in (face recognition)? How is consent obtained and what is the alternative for guests who decline?
Do you operate or participate in a loyalty programme? Is profiling consent obtained separately from booking consent?
Is guest data transferred to an international parent company or global reservation system? Are DPDP cross-border transfer safeguards in place?
Are DPAs in place with all OTA and booking platform partners that have access to guest data?
What is the procedure for deletion of foreign guest data after the Foreigners Order 3-year retention period?

evidence_requests.title: Guest data retention schedule and FRRO compliance documentation
evidence_requests.description: Retention schedule per data category citing specific legal mandate; deletion audit logs for guest identity data post-retention

evidence_requests.title: Loyalty programme consent mechanism
evidence_requests.description: Loyalty enrolment consent UI separate from booking; profiling opt-in evidence; opt-out test without penalty to loyalty balance

evidence_requests.title: OTA and booking platform DPAs
evidence_requests.description: Signed DPAs with all OTA platforms and booking aggregators; data processing scope and prohibition on secondary use

evidence_requests.title: Biometric check-in consent and alternative procedure
evidence_requests.description: Biometric consent form or digital mechanism; non-biometric alternative documentation; biometric deletion records

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Guest Data Retention Conflict Resolution — FRRO vs. DPDP
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Map all guest data categories against mandatory retention obligations (Foreigners Act, FRRO, MHA, PMLA) and DPDP storage limitation. Identify and document justified retention windows. Design deletion schedules for data not covered by legal mandates. Produce data retention policy with specific legal citations per data type.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Loyalty Programme Profiling Consent Architecture
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Review loyalty programme data flows and profiling depth. Assess consent mechanism for separability from core booking. Design DPDP-compliant loyalty consent architecture with granular profiling controls. Produce loyalty data governance policy and member communication for consent re-confirmation.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Cross-Border Guest Data Transfer Compliance for International Chains
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Map guest data flows to international parent companies and global reservation systems. Assess DPDP cross-border transfer compliance for each destination country. Design DPA framework for intra-group international data transfers. Produce data minimisation standard for cross-border guest data sharing.

legal.label: Legal / Professional Services
legal.description: Law firms, legal process outsourcing (LPO) companies, chartered accountants, and management consultancies processing client files, KYC documents, financial transaction records, and privileged communications. Attorney-client privilege intersects with DPDP transparency obligations.
sensitive_data_types: Client files and matter documentation
KYC documents (Aadhaar, PAN, passport, company registration)
Financial transaction records and beneficial ownership data
Legal strategy documents and privileged communications
Court filings and litigation records
Employee personnel files
AML/PMLA compliance records
Due diligence reports containing third-party personal data
applicable_regulations: Bar Council of India Rules on Professional Standards
Prevention of Money Laundering Act 2002 (PMLA) — mandatory KYC and record retention
SEBI regulations applicable to legal advisers in securities matters
Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA) — documentation requirements
legal.risk_notes: AMBER: Attorney-client privilege and professional confidentiality obligations coexist with DPDP transparency requirements — law firms must provide data principal notices and support rights requests while preserving privilege over legal strategy. PMLA mandates KYC data retention for 5 years after client relationship ends, which conflicts with DPDP erasure rights — document this conflict clearly and inform clients that PMLA retention obligations override DPDP erasure for KYC data. Cloud-hosted document management systems require robust DPAs and India-resident storage assessment.

additional_requirements.text: Law firms and professional services firms must provide clients with a DPDP-compliant data notice at matter inception covering: personal data collected, processing purpose, processors (cloud DMS, LPO partners), retention periods (including PMLA mandates), and rights. The notice must acknowledge where PMLA retention overrides erasure rights.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Client engagement letter or standalone data notice with all DPDP S.5 elements; notice provided before data collection; PMLA retention carve-out disclosed in notice; evidence translated to client's preferred language upon request
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.5 + PMLA 2002 + Bar Council of India Rules

additional_requirements.text: Client KYC data collected under PMLA obligations must be retained for the PMLA-mandated 5-year period post client relationship. After this period, KYC data must be deleted or anonymised. KYC data must not be used for business development, marketing analytics, or cross-sold to affiliates.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: KYC data retention schedule citing PMLA S.12; automated deletion after 5-year post-relationship period; evidence KYC data excluded from marketing or business development systems; PMLA compliance officer sign-off

additional_requirements.text: Cloud-based document management systems (DMS), legal research platforms, and LPO partners that process client personal data must be bound by signed DPAs. Contracts must specify India-resident data storage or explicit cross-border transfer safeguards.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: DPAs with all DMS providers, legal tech platforms, and LPO partners; India-resident storage clause or cross-border transfer safeguards; vendor access control records; evidence client data accessible only for stated matter work

additional_requirements.text: Clients may exercise DPDP rights including access and erasure. Firms must respond to access requests with a summary of personal data held, excluding privileged legal strategy. Erasure requests must be fulfilled for non-PMLA data; PMLA data must be retained with client informed of the legal basis.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Client data access request procedure; privilege review step before responding to access requests; erasure workflow distinguishing PMLA-retained data from DPDP-deletable data; client communication templates for partial erasure
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.11-12 + PMLA S.12 + Advocates Act

additional_requirements.text: Client files, privileged communications, and due diligence reports must be protected by role-based access control ensuring only matter-assigned personnel can access specific client files. External sharing of client documents must be via encrypted channels with link expiry.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: DMS RBAC configuration per matter; evidence client files accessible only to matter team; encrypted file sharing mechanism; link expiry controls; DMS access audit logs reviewed periodically
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.8(5) + Bar Council of India confidentiality rules

additional_requirements.text: Law firms and professional services firms must appoint a designated Privacy Officer (or DPO if SDF threshold met) responsible for DPDP compliance, client data notice maintenance, rights fulfilment, and breach response. The Privacy Officer must have independence from client-facing revenue functions.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Privacy Officer appointment record; Privacy Officer charter; independence from client-facing function; evidence Privacy Officer involved in new client intake data review; grievance contact details accessible to clients

intake_questions: Does your firm provide a DPDP data notice to clients at matter inception? Does it disclose PMLA retention obligations that may override erasure rights?
What cloud DMS or legal tech platforms process client personal data? Are DPAs in place with all of them, including India-resident storage or cross-border transfer clauses?
How are PMLA KYC retention obligations balanced against client erasure requests? Is there a documented procedure?
What process exists for client data access requests — how is privilege reviewed before disclosing matter data to a client?
Do you share client data with LPO partners or offshore legal support teams? Are DPAs in place?
Has a Privacy Officer been designated? Is there an accessible grievance mechanism for clients?

evidence_requests.title: Client data notice (engagement letter or standalone notice)
evidence_requests.description: Sample client data notice with DPDP S.5 elements; PMLA retention disclosure; evidence provided at matter inception

evidence_requests.title: DMS and LPO partner DPAs
evidence_requests.description: Signed DPAs with all document management systems and LPO partners; India-resident storage or cross-border transfer clause; access restriction to matter team

evidence_requests.title: KYC retention schedule and PMLA compliance evidence
evidence_requests.description: KYC retention schedule citing PMLA mandate; evidence of deletion after retention period; exclusion of KYC data from marketing systems

evidence_requests.title: DMS access controls and matter-team RBAC
evidence_requests.description: DMS RBAC configuration per matter; access audit logs; encrypted file sharing mechanism with link expiry

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Client Data Notice and PMLA-DPDP Conflict Resolution Workshop
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Review current client engagement letters and data notices for DPDP completeness. Map PMLA retention obligations against DPDP erasure rights and design disclosure language. Produce DPDP-compliant client data notice template. Design rights request procedure that respects privilege and PMLA obligations.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Legal Tech Vendor DPA and Cloud DMS Compliance Review
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Inventory all cloud DMS, legal research, and LPO platforms processing client data. Review existing DPAs for DPDP gaps. Design standard DPA template for legal professional services context. Assess India-resident storage vs. cross-border transfer compliance for each platform.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Client File Access Control and Privilege-Aware Rights Fulfilment
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Train matter teams on DPDP client data rights obligations and privilege review procedures. Design step-by-step rights fulfilment workflow distinguishing personal data from privileged strategy. Produce rights request handling SOP and client communication templates. Establish escalation path to Privacy Officer.

agritech.label: Agriculture / AgriTech
agritech.description: AgriTech platforms, agricultural input companies, commodity trading platforms, rural fintech, and government agricultural scheme administrators processing farmer land records, Aadhaar-linked subsidy data, crop data, and IoT farm sensor data.
sensitive_data_types: Farmer land records and cadastral data
Aadhaar-linked subsidy and PM-KISAN beneficiary data
Crop yield and soil health data
Financial inclusion and agricultural loan records
Farm IoT sensor data (soil moisture, weather, equipment telematics)
Commodity trading and price history data
Farmer cooperative and SHG membership data
Input purchase and usage history (seeds, fertilizers, pesticides)
applicable_regulations: PM-KISAN scheme operational guidelines (Aadhaar-linked beneficiary data)
NABARD data governance and farmer data guidelines
State Agricultural Produce Marketing Committee (APMC) Acts
National Food Security Act 2013 (ration/PDS beneficiary data)
agritech.risk_notes: AMBER: Farmer beneficiary data linked to Aadhaar for PM-KISAN, crop insurance, and PDS schemes involves a population with limited digital literacy and limited ability to exercise informed consent. AgriTech platforms collecting farm IoT data (soil sensors, drone imagery, equipment telematics) may build detailed farm profiles that reveal financial vulnerability — this creates profiling risk. Aadhaar-based authentication for government scheme data is subject to UIDAI regulations in addition to DPDP. Cross-border agricultural commodity trading data transfers require DPDP assessment.

additional_requirements.text: AgriTech platforms collecting farmer land records, crop data, and financial data must provide notices and obtain consent in the farmer's regional language. Consent obtained through intermediaries (input dealers, cooperative officers) must be verifiable and not structurally coerced.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Multilingual consent forms or digital consent in farmer's language; evidence consent mechanism accessible to low-digital-literacy users; intermediary consent collection guidelines; consent records with method and location documented

additional_requirements.text: PM-KISAN and government scheme beneficiary data accessed by AgriTech platforms through API integrations must be used only for the stated scheme-related purpose (eligibility verification, benefit delivery). Data must not be used for commercial crop advisory profiling, credit scoring, or input product marketing without separate farmer consent.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: PM-KISAN API data use restriction policy; technical controls preventing scheme beneficiary data use for commercial profiling; UIDAI authentication log review; evidence farmer data not used for marketing without separate consent
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.8(1) + Aadhaar Act S.8 + PM-KISAN operational guidelines

additional_requirements.text: Farm IoT sensor data (soil health monitors, weather stations, drone imagery, equipment telematics) must be collected only to the minimum granularity necessary for stated precision agriculture services. Raw IoT data must not be aggregated into farm financial vulnerability profiles shared with lenders without farmer consent.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: IoT data minimisation policy per device type; evidence raw IoT data not shared with lenders or insurers without consent; data flow documentation for farm sensor data; farmer consent for financial profiling using IoT data

additional_requirements.text: Cross-border agricultural commodity trading data transfers involving farmer identity or land records must comply with DPDP cross-border transfer requirements. International commodity exchanges and trading platforms receiving Indian farmer data must be covered by DPAs.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Inventory of cross-border farmer data transfers; DPAs with international commodity trading platforms; data minimisation for cross-border transfers (anonymise where possible); country compliance assessment

additional_requirements.text: Farmer financial data (crop loans, insurance claims, subsidies) and Aadhaar-linked records must be encrypted at rest and in transit. AgriTech platforms handling Aadhaar-linked data must comply with UIDAI security guidelines for authentication record protection.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Encryption configuration for farmer financial data; UIDAI authentication record security compliance; access controls for scheme beneficiary data; security assessment for AgriTech platform processing Aadhaar-linked data
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.8(5) + Aadhaar Act + UIDAI guidelines

additional_requirements.text: Farmers must be able to access and request correction of their land records, crop data, and scheme eligibility data held by AgriTech platforms. Rights request mechanisms must be accessible in regional languages and via voice/non-digital channels where digital literacy is low.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Farmer data access request mechanism in regional languages; non-digital access request channel (helpline); SLA for responding to farmer rights requests; evidence correction cascades to land records systems and scheme portals

intake_questions: Does the platform integrate with PM-KISAN, crop insurance, or other government scheme databases? On what basis is farmer Aadhaar data accessed, and is it used only for scheme purposes?
How is farmer consent obtained? Is the consent mechanism accessible to farmers with low digital literacy, and is it available in regional languages?
What IoT sensors or drone systems collect farm data? Is this data minimised, and is it shared with lenders or insurers — if so, with farmer consent?
Do you share farmer land or financial data with international commodity trading platforms? Are DPDP cross-border transfer safeguards in place?
What is the security posture for Aadhaar-linked farmer data? Is UIDAI authentication log access controlled?
How can farmers access, correct, or request deletion of their data? Is there a non-digital rights request channel?

evidence_requests.title: Multilingual farmer consent mechanism documentation
evidence_requests.description: Consent forms or UI in regional languages; intermediary consent collection guidelines; sample consent records

evidence_requests.title: PM-KISAN/scheme API data use restriction policy and controls
evidence_requests.description: Data use restriction policy for government scheme API data; technical controls preventing commercial secondary use; UIDAI authentication logs

evidence_requests.title: Farm IoT data minimisation policy and lender/insurer data sharing controls
evidence_requests.description: IoT data minimisation policy per device; evidence IoT data not shared with financial institutions without farmer consent

evidence_requests.title: Farmer rights request mechanism (including non-digital channel)
evidence_requests.description: Farmer data access mechanism; regional language support; helpline or non-digital channel documentation; SLA for rights request fulfilment

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Farmer Consent Design for Low-Digital-Literacy Contexts
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Review consent mechanisms across farmer touchpoints (app, dealer, cooperative, IVR). Assess against DPDP Section 5-6 requirements for regional language and accessibility. Design DPDP-compliant consent approach for low-digital-literacy populations. Produce multilingual consent templates and intermediary consent collection guidelines.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Government Scheme API Data Governance and UIDAI Compliance
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Review all government scheme API integrations (PM-KISAN, crop insurance, PDS). Assess UIDAI compliance for Aadhaar-linked authentication. Map data use restrictions against DPDP purpose limitation. Design technical controls preventing secondary commercial use of scheme data. Produce scheme data governance policy.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Farm IoT Data Minimisation and Responsible Profiling Framework
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Inventory all IoT devices and farm data streams. Design data minimisation standards per device type. Assess financial profiling risk for IoT-derived farm data shared with lenders and insurers. Produce farmer consent framework for financial data sharing and responsible data use policy for AgriTech operations.

realestate.label: Real Estate / PropTech
realestate.description: Builders, developers, real estate agents, PropTech platforms, co-living operators, and property management companies processing buyer/seller KYC, RERA-registered project data, tenant records, and PMLA-mandated transaction documents.
sensitive_data_types: Buyer and seller Aadhaar and PAN data
Property title documents and ownership records
Financial transaction records and payment proofs
Biometric building access logs
Tenant KYC documents and police verification records
RERA-registered project data and buyer agreements
NRI buyer identity and overseas address data
Beneficial ownership and anti-benami transaction records
applicable_regulations: Prevention of Money Laundering Act 2002 (PMLA) — property transaction KYC
FEMA regulations for NRI property transactions
realestate.risk_notes: AMBER: Real estate transactions involve PMLA KYC obligations that mandate 5-year record retention post-transaction — this directly conflicts with DPDP erasure rights for buyer and seller KYC data. Document this conflict and disclose it to data principals. RERA disclosure obligations require project data publication that may contain personal data — assess what is publicly disclosed. PropTech platforms acting as data processors for multiple developers require DPAs with each developer. Biometric access systems in residential buildings require explicit resident consent.

additional_requirements.text: Buyer and seller KYC data (Aadhaar, PAN, financial records) collected under PMLA obligations must be retained for the PMLA-mandated 5-year period post-transaction. After this period, KYC data must be deleted or anonymised. KYC data must not be used for marketing, lead generation, or sold to third parties.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Transaction KYC retention schedule citing PMLA mandate; evidence of automated deletion or anonymisation after 5-year period; technical controls preventing KYC use for marketing; PMLA compliance officer records

additional_requirements.text: Real estate developers and agents must provide buyers and sellers with a DPDP-compliant data notice disclosing: KYC data collected, PMLA retention obligations (including disclosure that erasure rights are limited for PMLA data), processors (PropTech platforms, title verification services), and rights available.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Client data notice in sale agreement or standalone document; PMLA retention carve-out disclosed; notice provided before data collection; RERA registration number referenced in notice
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.5 + RERA S.4 + PMLA

additional_requirements.text: Biometric access control systems in residential buildings, co-living spaces, and commercial properties must obtain explicit written consent from residents and employees before enrolment. Non-biometric access alternatives must always be available.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Biometric consent form or digital consent mechanism; evidence non-biometric alternative (card, PIN) available; biometric data deletion process for departed residents; biometric system vendor DPA

additional_requirements.text: PropTech platforms acting as data processors for multiple real estate developers must execute separate DPAs with each developer. The platform must not combine client data across developer engagements or use data from one developer's clients for another developer's marketing.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: DPAs with each developer client; data isolation controls between developer engagements; evidence no cross-developer data sharing; audit logs confirming data segregation by developer

additional_requirements.text: NRI buyer and seller transaction data transferred to overseas banks, foreign exchange dealers, or international parent companies must comply with DPDP cross-border transfer requirements and FEMA data handling provisions.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Inventory of NRI transaction data transferred abroad; DPAs with foreign banks and forex dealers receiving NRI data; FEMA compliance for cross-border property transaction data; data minimisation for international transfers

additional_requirements.text: RERA-mandated project disclosure data published on RERA portals must be reviewed to ensure personal data of buyers, investors, or employees is not unnecessarily included in public disclosures. Personal data in RERA filings must be limited to what the regulation explicitly requires.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: RERA filing data review records; evidence personal data minimised in public RERA disclosures; process for reviewing project disclosure documents before RERA portal submission

intake_questions: Does the organisation collect buyer or seller KYC under PMLA obligations? Is the 5-year retention mandate documented and implemented?
Are buyers and sellers provided a DPDP data notice that discloses PMLA retention obligations and limitation of erasure rights?
Do residential or commercial properties have biometric access systems? Is explicit resident consent obtained with a non-biometric alternative available?
Does the PropTech platform process data for multiple developers? Are separate DPAs in place per developer, with data isolation controls?
Is NRI buyer transaction data transferred to overseas financial institutions? Are DPDP cross-border transfer safeguards and FEMA compliance in place?
Are RERA project disclosure documents reviewed for personal data minimisation before submission to the RERA portal?

evidence_requests.title: Transaction KYC retention schedule and PMLA compliance evidence
evidence_requests.description: KYC retention schedule citing PMLA mandate; automated deletion evidence post-retention; exclusion from marketing systems

evidence_requests.title: Client data notice in sale agreement
evidence_requests.description: Sample client data notice with PMLA retention disclosure; evidence provided before data collection

evidence_requests.title: Biometric access consent and non-biometric alternative documentation
evidence_requests.description: Biometric consent forms; evidence non-biometric alternative available; biometric data deletion records for departed residents

evidence_requests.title: PropTech developer client DPAs and data isolation evidence
evidence_requests.description: DPAs with each developer client; data segregation controls; audit logs confirming no cross-developer data sharing

advisory_clinic_templates.title: PMLA-DPDP Conflict Resolution for Real Estate Transaction Data
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Map all KYC and transaction data categories against PMLA retention mandates and DPDP erasure rights. Design retention schedules with legal citations. Draft disclosure language for client notices. Produce PMLA-DPDP conflict resolution policy and buyer communication template.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: PropTech Multi-Developer Data Isolation and DPA Governance
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Review PropTech platform architecture for data isolation between developer clients. Assess DPA coverage across all developer engagements. Design data isolation controls and DPA standard template for real estate context. Establish developer onboarding privacy checklist.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Biometric Building Access Consent and Resident Data Rights
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Review biometric access systems across all properties. Design resident and employee consent architecture with non-biometric alternatives. Train facilities and security teams on DPDP obligations for biometric data. Produce biometric governance policy and resident communication templates.

energy.label: Energy / Utilities
energy.description: Electricity distribution companies (DISCOMs), power generators, oil and gas companies, gas distribution networks, and renewable energy operators processing smart meter data, customer billing records, and employee health and safety data at critical infrastructure facilities.
sensitive_data_types: Smart meter consumption data (granular energy usage patterns)
Customer billing and payment records
Employee health, medical fitness, and safety incident data
Biometric access logs for power plants and critical installations
Operational technology (OT/SCADA) system access and activity logs
Pipeline and grid operational data with employee identifiers
Environmental compliance and incident reporting data
Renewable energy producer and prosumer data
applicable_regulations: Petroleum and Natural Gas Regulatory Board (PNGRB) regulations
National Smart Grid Mission (NSGM) data security guidelines
CERT-In Critical Information Infrastructure (CII) security directives
energy.risk_notes: AMBER: Smart meter data at granular intervals (15-minute reads) reveals intimate details of occupant behaviour, daily routines, and presence/absence at premises — this is personal data requiring consent for processing beyond billing. DISCOMs and energy companies operating Critical Information Infrastructure (CII) have additional CERT-In security obligations that overlap with DPDP security safeguards. Employee monitoring at plant sites (biometric, CCTV, HSE tracking) requires employment-basis justification under DPDP. SCADA/OT system data that incidentally captures personal data must be assessed for DPDP scope.

additional_requirements.text: Smart meter consumption data collected at granular intervals (15-minute or hourly reads) must be used primarily for billing and network balancing. Commercial use of granular consumption data for analytics, targeted energy products, or third-party sharing requires explicit customer consent separate from the metering service agreement.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Smart meter data use policy limiting primary use to billing and network operations; explicit opt-in consent for commercial analytics use; evidence granular consumption data not sold to third parties without consent; NSGM data security compliance assessment
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.6 + NSGM guidelines + SERC smart metering regulations

additional_requirements.text: SCADA and OT systems that interface with employee access control, health and safety monitoring, or customer management systems must be assessed for personal data flows. Personal data must not traverse unsecured OT networks. OT/IT convergence points must have documented data protection controls.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: OT/IT network segmentation assessment; personal data flow mapping for SCADA-connected systems; security controls at OT/IT convergence points; CERT-In CII security assessment records
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.8(5) + CERT-In CII directives + Electricity Act

additional_requirements.text: Biometric access controls (fingerprint, facial recognition) at power plants, substations, and critical energy installations must be accompanied by employee notice disclosing data collection, purpose, retention period, and access controls. Biometric data must be deleted upon employment termination.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Biometric notice in employment contract or standalone document; biometric data retention policy; deletion procedure at employment end; biometric system vendor DPA; evidence biometric systems not installed in prohibited areas

additional_requirements.text: Employee health, medical fitness, and occupational safety incident data collected under Factories Act and occupational health requirements must be used only for safety and compliance purposes. This data must not be used for performance evaluation, insurance underwriting, or shared with third parties without employee consent.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Occupational health data use policy; evidence safety/health data excluded from HR performance systems; DPAs with occupational health service providers; employee consent for health data sharing beyond statutory reporting
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.8(1) + Factories Act 1948 + Mines Act 1952

additional_requirements.text: Cross-border energy trading data involving customer consumption records or employee data transferred to international power exchanges or joint venture partners must comply with DPDP cross-border transfer requirements.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Cross-border energy trading data transfer inventory; DPAs with international power exchange partners; data minimisation for cross-border operational data transfers; country compliance assessment
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.16 + CERC regulations on cross-border electricity trade

additional_requirements.text: Energy companies designated as Critical Information Infrastructure (CII) operators must align their DPDP security safeguards programme with CERT-In CII security directives. The DPO must coordinate with the CISO for CERT-In compliance activities that overlap with DPDP breach notification and security obligations.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: CERT-In CII designation status and compliance assessment; DPO-CISO coordination mechanism; DPDP breach notification integration with CERT-In incident reporting; evidence overlapping security controls are jointly maintained
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.8(5)-S.8(6) + CERT-In IT Act S.70B

intake_questions: What granularity of smart meter data is collected (15-minute, hourly, daily reads)? Is granular consumption data used for purposes beyond billing, and on what consent basis?
Do SCADA or OT systems interface with HR, access control, or customer management systems? Have personal data flows from OT systems been assessed?
Are biometric access systems deployed at critical installations? Are employees notified and is deletion at employment end implemented?
What occupational health data is collected under Factories Act requirements? Is it separated from HR performance systems?
Is the organisation designated as Critical Information Infrastructure (CII) by CERT-In? How do CII obligations intersect with DPDP security and breach notification?
Is customer or employee data transferred to international power exchange partners or joint venture entities abroad?

evidence_requests.title: Smart meter data use policy and commercial analytics consent
evidence_requests.description: Smart meter data use policy limiting primary use to billing; commercial analytics opt-in consent mechanism; NSGM compliance assessment

evidence_requests.title: OT/SCADA personal data flow assessment and CII security evidence
evidence_requests.description: OT/IT convergence personal data flow mapping; CERT-In CII security assessment; segmentation evidence

evidence_requests.title: Employee biometric notice and deletion procedure
evidence_requests.description: Biometric notice in employment documentation; biometric retention policy; deletion records at employment end; biometric system DPA

evidence_requests.title: Occupational health data separation controls
evidence_requests.description: Occupational health data use policy; evidence exclusion from HR performance systems; DPAs with occupational health providers

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Smart Meter Data Privacy and Consent Governance Workshop
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Map all smart meter data flows from meter to billing to analytics systems. Assess granularity against DPDP consent requirements. Design consent architecture for commercial analytics use of consumption data. Produce smart metering data governance policy aligned to NSGM and SERC requirements.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: OT/SCADA Personal Data Assessment and CERT-In DPDP Alignment
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Conduct personal data flow assessment for all OT-IT convergence points. Map CERT-In CII security obligations against DPDP security safeguards. Design integrated security and privacy controls for critical energy infrastructure. Produce OT personal data governance policy.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Employee Health and Biometric Data Compliance Briefing
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Review biometric access systems and occupational health data handling at plant sites. Assess compliance with DPDP employee data notice obligations. Design biometric data governance policy including deletion at employment end. Produce occupational health data separation controls and DPDP-Factories Act compliance guide.

logistics.label: Logistics / Supply Chain
logistics.description: Logistics operators, freight forwarders, e-commerce fulfilment providers, courier companies, and supply chain technology platforms processing shipment tracking data, driver/employee records, customer delivery addresses, and customs KYC documents.
sensitive_data_types: Customer delivery addresses and contact data
Shipment tracking and delivery route data
Driver and delivery executive records (ID, location, biometric)
Customs and EXIM KYC documents (importer/exporter identity)
Biometric warehouse and depot access logs
Vehicle telematics and GPS tracking data
Employee health and safety records
International freight partner personnel data
applicable_regulations: DGFT (Directorate General of Foreign Trade) export documentation requirements
EXIM Bank and customs authority data requirements
logistics.risk_notes: AMBER: Driver and delivery executive tracking (continuous GPS, performance monitoring) creates significant consent and transparency obligations — employees must be informed about monitoring scope and purpose. Customer delivery address data at scale is high-value personal data that must be minimised, retained only as long as needed for delivery, and not shared with marketing partners. Customs and EXIM KYC data retention obligations (typically 5 years under Customs Act) conflict with DPDP erasure rights — document and disclose this conflict. DPAs with international freight partners require careful cross-border transfer assessment.

additional_requirements.text: Driver and delivery executive continuous GPS tracking and performance monitoring must be disclosed in the employment or service agreement. The notice must specify: what is tracked, when tracking is active, who has access to tracking data, retention period, and the purpose (safety, route optimisation, proof of delivery).
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Employment or service agreement clause disclosing GPS tracking and performance monitoring scope; evidence notice provided before tracking commenced; tracking system vendor DPA; driver access to their own tracking data
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.5-6 + Motor Vehicles Act

additional_requirements.text: Customer delivery address and contact data must be retained only for the period necessary to complete delivery and handle post-delivery disputes (typically 30-90 days). Delivery addresses must not be used for marketing profiling or sold to third parties. Aggregated delivery route analytics must not be linkable back to individual customer addresses.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Customer delivery data retention schedule; automated deletion after delivery + dispute window; evidence delivery addresses excluded from marketing systems; anonymisation approach for route analytics

additional_requirements.text: Customs and EXIM documentation KYC data (importer/exporter Aadhaar, PAN, IEC) retained under Customs Act obligations must be retained only for the legally mandated period (5 years post-shipment). After this period, KYC data must be deleted or anonymised. Data must not be used for commercial profiling.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Customs KYC retention schedule citing Customs Act and DGFT mandates; evidence of automated deletion post-retention; KYC data exclusion from commercial profiling systems; EXIM compliance officer records
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.8(3) + Customs Act 1962 + DGFT regulations

additional_requirements.text: Biometric access control systems at warehouses and distribution centres must obtain explicit consent from warehouse employees before enrolment. Non-biometric access alternatives must be available. Biometric data must be deleted upon employment termination.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Warehouse biometric consent forms; evidence non-biometric access alternative (card, PIN) available; biometric deletion records at employment end; biometric system vendor DPA

additional_requirements.text: International freight and logistics partners (foreign freight forwarders, shipping lines, customs brokers in other countries) that receive Indian shipment tracking data, customer consignee data, or employee data must be bound by DPAs. Cross-border transfers must comply with DPDP cross-border transfer requirements.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: DPAs with all international freight partners and foreign customs brokers; cross-border transfer inventory for shipment and customer data; country compliance assessment; data minimisation for international transfers
additional_requirements.sector_law: DPDP Act S.16 + Multi-Modal Transportation Act

additional_requirements.text: Shipment tracking systems, last-mile delivery platforms, and warehouse management systems (WMS) must implement role-based access control and encryption for customer and employee personal data. Driver location data must not be accessible to unauthorised employees beyond dispatchers and safety officers.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: Shipment tracking system RBAC; WMS access controls; driver location data access restriction to authorised roles; encryption of customer delivery data at rest and in transit; security assessment for core logistics platforms

additional_requirements.text: E-commerce clients and marketplace partners whose customers' delivery data is processed by the logistics provider must be covered by DPAs designating the logistics provider as a data processor. These DPAs must specify purpose limitation to delivery fulfilment only.
additional_requirements.evidence_expectation: DPAs with all e-commerce clients and marketplace partners; purpose limitation clause restricting use to delivery fulfilment; evidence customer data not used beyond delivery; DPA inventory with renewal dates

intake_questions: Are drivers and delivery executives informed of GPS tracking and performance monitoring in their employment or service agreement? What is tracked and for how long is it retained?
What is the retention period for customer delivery address data after order completion, and is it deleted automatically after the dispute window?
Is customs and EXIM KYC data retained for the Customs Act 5-year mandate? Is deletion automated after this period, and is KYC data excluded from commercial systems?
Are biometric access systems deployed at warehouses? Is explicit consent obtained and is a non-biometric alternative available?
Are all international freight partners and foreign customs brokers bound by DPAs? Have DPDP cross-border transfer safeguards been assessed?
Are DPAs in place with all e-commerce clients whose customer delivery data is processed by the logistics operation?

evidence_requests.title: Driver tracking disclosure in employment/service agreement
evidence_requests.description: Employment or service agreement clause disclosing GPS and performance tracking; evidence provided before tracking commenced; driver DPA or acknowledgement

evidence_requests.title: Customer delivery data retention schedule and deletion evidence
evidence_requests.description: Retention schedule for delivery addresses; automated deletion after delivery + dispute window; exclusion from marketing systems

evidence_requests.title: International freight partner DPAs and cross-border transfer inventory
evidence_requests.description: DPAs with all international logistics partners and customs brokers; cross-border transfer inventory; country compliance assessment

evidence_requests.title: E-commerce client DPAs designating logistics provider as data processor
evidence_requests.description: DPAs with e-commerce and marketplace clients; purpose limitation to delivery fulfilment; DPA inventory

evidence_requests.title: Warehouse biometric consent and non-biometric alternative documentation
evidence_requests.description: Warehouse biometric consent forms; non-biometric alternative evidence; biometric deletion records at employment end

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Driver and Delivery Executive Monitoring Transparency Workshop
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Audit all driver and delivery executive monitoring systems (GPS, performance scoring, idle time tracking, delivery photo capture). Review disclosure adequacy in employment and service agreements. Design DPDP-compliant monitoring transparency framework. Produce driver monitoring policy and service agreement clause templates.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: Customer Delivery Data Minimisation and Retention Design
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Map all customer delivery data flows from order receipt through delivery completion and dispute resolution. Design data minimisation standards and automated deletion schedules. Assess exclusion of delivery addresses from marketing and analytics systems. Produce customer delivery data governance policy.

advisory_clinic_templates.title: International Freight Partner DPDP Compliance Programme
advisory_clinic_templates.guidance: Map all international shipment data flows to foreign freight forwarders, customs brokers, and shipping lines. Assess DPDP cross-border transfer compliance per partner and destination country. Design DPA template for international logistics context. Establish cross-border transfer register and annual partner review process.
